data.title: Microsoft: This Might Be The Top
data.description:
Although Microsoft’s (NASDAQ:MSFT) stock has slightly increased since we published our latest article on the business in November, we continue to think that the company is overvalued at the current price. We also think that Microsoft’s desire to increase its capital spending to record levels to expand its AI capabilities could backfire due to the rising macro risks that can undermine the potential of its AI projects. As such, we continue to rate the company as a SELL.

Some Good Things To Consider First

While we’re bearish about Microsoft right now, it’s still important to note that the company nevertheless has some good things going for it right now. Just a few weeks ago, Microsoft reported its Q3 results, which positively surprised the market as revenues of $70.06 billion were above expectations by $1.61 billion, while GAAP EPS of $3.46 was also above the consensus by $0.24.

The productivity and business processes division managed to generate $29.9 billion in revenues, up 10% Y/Y, while the personal computing division generated $13.4 billion in revenues, up 6% Y/Y.

Back in January, Gartner forecasted that the global IT spending could reach record levels in 2025 and spending on software is forecasted to grow at a double-digit rate. If that’s the case, then Microsoft’s core business might have additional room for growth.

Also, the cloud business showed exceptional results in Q1, as its revenues of $26.8 billion were up 21% Y/Y. Considering the strengths of Azure, it’s possible for it to continue to show a decent performance in the foreseeable future.

The Macro Risks Are Real

Despite all the recent positive developments, there’s a case to be made that Microsoft’s downside is more significant, and its stock has room to fall.

For starters, Gartner’s forecast was released back in January before the global trade war by the Trump administration was initially launched. While there has been a pause on the implementation of reciprocal tariffs on most countries by the Trump administration in April, there’s still uncertainty about what’s going to happen next and what the updated trade policy might look like in the future.

Just recently, President Trump announced that he’s going to recommend a 50% tariff on goods imported from the European Union starting June 1, which is before the date when the 90-day pause should expire. Such uncertainty regarding the trade policy makes it harder for businesses and consumers to forecast their spending, which might hurt economic activity.

During the latest conference call, the retail giant Walmart (WMT) already announced the possibility of higher prices for the products that it sells due to the current trade policy. Also, consumer sentiment has now fallen to its 3-year low, while the Federal Reserve hasn’t fully dismissed the stagflation scenario in the latest meeting.

When it comes to software spending in particular, the report that was published by the Boston Consulting Group earlier this month showed that there has been a significant decline in the desire of CIOs to increase their IT budgets this year, mostly because of tariffs. The majority of CIOs now also believe that the recession is likely this year. As a result of this, Gartner’s January forecast on spending might not materialize by the end of the year as risks increase. Therefore, while Microsoft had a great performance in Q3, which is also a period where no major tariffs were announced, there’s now a risk that the sales might suffer in Q4 and beyond as businesses begin to adapt to the new macro environment.

Are We In A Bubble?

Besides the macro issues, there’s a possibility that there’s an overcapacity of data centers, and the expected record spending on AI-related initiatives by Microsoft and its peers might lead to an overbuilding within the industry and depress future earnings.

In late March, it was reported that Microsoft had decided to abandon some data center projects due to the oversupply. Then Alibaba’s (BABA) CEO Joe Tsai warned of a potential AI data center bubble, while Wells Fargo reported that Amazon is pausing some of its lease commitments as well. The news from China also indicates that due to the aggressive build-out, many data centers now sit idly and companies have started to sell their GPUs so that they can somewhat offset the oversupply issues.

While there is no way to figure out how many data centers around the globe are unused, the fact that the semiconductor sales have been below their peak levels for a few months might suggest that the oversupply issues are real.

The problem though is that even after abandoning some of its own projects a few months ago, Microsoft’s management during the latest conference call committed once again to spending the record CapEx this year.

This could become a major problem for Microsoft and its peers as it could lead to overbuilding within the industry, which already faces oversupply issues. Let’s not forget that in the late 1990s, the telecom industry also poured capital into massive projects only to yield minimal or no returns at all. As the former Under Secretary of Commerce for Economic Affairs Mark Domes noted in one of his works:

… the telecom service sector went on a capital expenditure binge … capital investment by publicly traded telecom service companies rose sharply in the late 1990s, starting at $47 billion in 1995 and peaking at $121 billion in 2000. Since then, however, the telecom service industry landscape is littered with the wrecks of overly optimistic expectations, as witnessed by the bankruptcies by WorldCom, Global Crossing, and numerous smaller firms.

What’s also important to note is that the overbuilding also tends to depress the future earnings. Here’s how American economists Robin Greenwood and Samuel Hanson explain this:

… heavy investment during booms predictably depresses future earnings and the price of capital, leading prices to overshoot their rational-expectations levels … particularly in markets—such as industries with long time-to-build delays—where feedback is delayed and learning is slow.

This shows that Microsoft’s upside might be limited, as the record capital spending in the current environment could yield limited returns.

The Intrinsic Value of Microsoft

The rising macro risks and the expected higher capital spending prompted us to change several assumptions in our valuation model, which last year showed Microsoft’s intrinsic value to be $324.16 per share.

Our valuation model on Microsoft is updated at a time when the company trades at $458.17 per share. The cash and total debt numbers that are presented in the new model have been taken from the recent earnings report, while the perpetual growth rate here is 3%, the same as in the previous model. The 3% also makes the most sense, as it’s close to the historical GDP and inflation rates.

The discount rate in the model is 7.66%. We figured it out by calculating Microsoft’s cost of debt using mostly its TTM results, and by calculating the company’s cost of equity where the risk-free rate is 4.52%, beta is 1.02, and the market return rate is 7.77%.

In the table below, we still forecast the growth of Microsoft’s sales and EBIT margin as the company is less likely to be significantly impacted by tariffs in comparison to other companies from the Magnificent Seven group like Apple (AAPL) or Amazon (AMZN). However, the growth rate nevertheless is expected to be slightly lower in comparison to the previous model. This is because there’s a possibility that the potential negative impact of the global trade war risks decreasing the demand for the company’s products and services. The sales assumptions for the next couple of years are also not that far away from the overall consensus.

The assumptions for the tax rate have been lowered to 15%, which could become a new standard corporate rate in the United States, while the assumptions for the D&A and the change in net working capital mostly remained the same as before.

At the same time, the CapEx expectations have been greatly increased. This is because Microsoft’s management earlier this year said that CapEx in Q3 and Q4 should be similar to the Q2 levels. In Q3, the CapEx of $16.7 billion was only slightly above the Q2 levels. At the same time, during the latest conference call the management reiterated that the expectations so far haven’t changed significantly but CapEx in Q4 is still expected to be up Q/Q. We have reflected these capital spending expectations for the current fiscal year in our model.

In the end, our valuation model showed that Microsoft’s enterprise value is $2.35 trillion, its equity value is $2.32 trillion, and its intrinsic value is $311.60 per share, down around 32% from the current price.

Final Thoughts

The amount of risks that Microsoft faces has been on the rise and will likely continue to increase due to the worsening of the macro environment. The company’s desire to drastically increase its capital spending in the current environment is also a bad idea, in our opinion, since the return on that spending might yield limited returns. As such, Microsoft’s shares, in our opinion, have room to fall, especially since the company remains overvalued at the current price. That is why Microsoft remains a SELL for us.

data.title: Stellantis: Margin Recovery Catalysts, Initiate Buy
data.description:
Key thesis

We initiate coverage on Stellantis N.V. (NYSE: NYSE:STLA) with a Buy rating and a $14 PT. STLA designs, manufactures, and markets a broad range of automobiles, commercial vehicles, and related parts and services in global markets. Our Buy thesis is based on our FY25E EBITDA of €22.4bn and FY26E EBITDA of €23.3bn, +5.6% and +5% ahead of consensus, on product launches, execution of the €10bn cost program, and accelerating Latin America momentum, up 150bps of share in 1Q25. We see a significant market disconnect on STLA's earnings power, with shares trading at 0.73x forward EV/EBITDA (EV based on prevailing market cap and net debt, and our in-house FY26E EBITDA forecast), vs. peers. Our PT uses 1.70x FY26E EV/EBITDA, which is still conservative relative to history/peers, but reflects both operational progress made and embedded structural risk.

NA pricing normalization and diminishing dealer inventory provide the backdrop for margin inflection and cost absorption into 2H25, alongside vehicle launches, including the Citroën C3, Dodge Charger, and Jeep Wagoneer S, providing incremental upside as they reach full availability. Tariff policy uncertainty and CEO transition are priced into our valuation. We see a favorable R/R, with real catalysts for earnings outperformance and valuation normalization over our forecast period.

Stellantis: Modeling EBITDA Outperformance with Resilient Upstream Insulation

We model FY25E/FY26E EBITDA of €22.4bn/€23.3bn (+5.6%/+5% above the Street consensus) as disciplined execution on €10bn cost-out program & high-impact model launches catalyzing earnings resilience. Key cost-out milestones include the capture of €2.5–3bn of annualized run-rate savings by end-2025. We expect increased visibility over ≤€650–750mn quarterly cost-out captured in SG&A/procurement, with concrete progress already factored into a 29% US dealer inventory reduction in H2 2024. We should see a steady decline of fixed costs & SG&A-to-sales ratios in quarterly P&Ls, with visible milestones including inventory normalization, production schedules & lower recourse to cash.

On the product side, management forecasts targeting >300k units of incremental annual volume (AV) impact from key ramps (Citroën C3, Dodge Charger, Jeep Wagoneer S) by end-2025 guide forecasts of group market share stabilization in Europe & an evidenced mix shift towards B/C segments. We model robust product ramps to more than offset industry headwinds, contributing >€2bn to group FY25E revenues and, combined with scale & platform sharing, at least 60–80bps to group EBITDA margin by FY26E due to the full-year difference, including acceleration in Smart Car platform and midsize BEVs.

The full-year impact of key launches supports the conviction that Stellantis can deliver material margin recovery against a backdrop of industry-wide headwinds. The improving regional portfolio, notably South America, with Q1 2025 market share up 1.5ppt to 23.8%, should deliver resilience to the group and offset the North American AM piece pricing resets to support above-consensus IAS 17 core earnings.

Inventories now at healthy levels across regions should help drive a continued improvement in WC and heightened quarterly visibility. Risks from US tariffs and CEO succession are acknowledged, although conservatively incorporated into our forecasts. We believe to have cost-out milestones tracked and bottom-up analyses of the volumes/margin impact from key race launches to provide visibility & opacify Street estimates on core EBITDA & EPS line through FY26.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $174.24B 14 $182.14B 14
EBITDA $21.21B 14 $22.17B 14
Net Income $5.92B 14 $7.80B 14
EPS $1.99 4 $2.62 3
SG&A Expenses $9.94B 14 $10.39B 14

Cost Takeout and Margin Inflection: The Key to a Re-Rating

A credible margin inflection arguably remains the most important catalyst for STLA at this stage, as the group emerged from a wobbly 2024 (-64% adj. Op. income; AOI margin bottoming at the bottom of the 5.5–7% range). The €10bn cost takeout is not just a headline number: its tangible delivery can already be seen in the aggressive headcount restructuring (which was €1.6bn in 2024, and we expect to taper going forward, providing a front-loaded benefit to recurring cost savings). Equally important, STLA's normalization of NA dealer inventory levels (from 430k midyear to 304k YE, vs 330k target) has reset the baseline for production and pricing discipline, removing the need for margin-dilutive incentives and forced production cuts.

This operational reset is set to manufacturing utilization and FCF, with management explicitly guiding to mid-single-digit AOI margins and a return to positive industrial FCF in 2025 as plant loading and cost absorption improve with the return of volumes in H2. In our view, successful cost discipline and normalized inventory levels are the bedrock not only for near-term margin inflection but for a re-rating of STLA as it demonstrates the ability to generate sustainable cash and earnings above depressed expectations.

Valuation

Our $14 PT is derived by applying a 1.70x EV/EBITDA multiple to our FY26E EBITDA forecast of €23.3bn, reflecting a balanced mix of operational upside and risk discount. The target multiple is materially below the historical median (2.0x) and below the peer group median (2.3x) based on a trailing 8-year dataset across global OEMs (1st–99th percentile range), sourced from FMP. This implies a historical/relative percentile (1st, 0th) near absolute trough and a significant margin of safety. The multiple is warranted by our conviction in company-specific catalysts around the execution of the €10bn cost-reduction plan, and positive follow-through from new model launches, which underpins our above-consensus FY26E EBITDA expansion of +4.0%. These structural cost improvements, combined with North American pricing normalization and ongoing inventory drawdowns, justify a measured normalization in the company's multiple without dismissing ongoing uncertainty around tariffs and succession planning.

Importantly, while market narratives remain focused on Stellantis' cyclical exposure and headline EV transition risk, we argue that the Street consensus underestimates the scale of margin leverage from cost disciplines already in place, an operational dynamic largely overlooked but now apparent in Latin America's market share gains. Ultimately, our valuation captures the interplay of risk adj. and margin catalyst, offering a compelling R/R anchored in tangible operational catalysts.

Downside risks to our Buy recommendation

While we see a compelling margin recovery and multiple re-rating opportunities in Stellantis, our Buy thesis could be undermined by lingering execution headwinds or macro developments. Key risks remain under close watch:

• Tariff regime uncertainty and trade policy evolution, particularly in the US, with newly implemented and prospective tariffs on imported vehicles and key inputs threatening NA operations, pricing flexibility, or cost structure, with management itself citing suspended visibility and continuing “tariff-related uncertainties” as a reason for withdrawing guidance for FY25E.

• Execution of a restructuring program and labor relations, with the group’s ambitious €10bn cost reduction program at risk of stalling in the face of labor tensions (notably in the US, where dealer dissatisfaction is rising and profit warnings/revived union strike threats are coming out in the media), especially if management's new approach/leadership does not succeed in calming unrest and segment overcapacity.

• Fierce EV transition ahead, with accelerating Chinese EV imports in Europe/NA and low elasticity for BEVs in the face of policy flux and slower-than-anticipated electrified model growth, constituting a structural risk requiring additional pricing action or capping volume/margin expansion if not proactively managed.

In summary, if tariffs unexpectedly escalate, inventory normalization falters, or the competitive/EV transition landscape worsens, our forecast of rapid EBITDA outperformance and re-rating is at risk. Management’s ability to navigate these cross-currents, retain a disciplined cost trajectory, and claw back NA volume share are the most important areas to watch for risk to our Buy thesis.

Conclusion

We initiate with a Buy on STLA, given the rare alignment of NT margin catalysts and Op momentum (including accelerating leverage from cost-discipline and new model launches) that we believe is materially underappreciated by the market, alongside unbalanced conservatism around a valuation framework that offers a favorable risk/reward vs. cyclicality and structural risks. We recommend using volatility to add to exposure, with management cost execution and NA share recovery providing the visibility to upside.

data.title: TD Bank Beats Q2 Earnings, Pledges More Buybacks (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The Toronto-Dominion Bank (NYSE:TD)(TSX:TD:CA) released its second quarter earnings Yesterday. The release beat analyst estimates on revenue as well as on earnings, with revenue coming in a full $1.7 billion ahead of estimates. The company's U.S. retail segment was again a weak point, with earnings down 76%. However, the results in all other segments--Canadian retail, insurance, and capital markets--were excellent.

TD's Q2 release came following several months of TD stock outperforming the broader market. The stock was already up for the year when its earnings release came out; it is now up 27% year to date.

I was pretty optimistic heading into TD's earnings release. I knew that the bank had good prospects in several segments, such as wholesale banking (i.e. investment banking) and Canadian retail. Investment banking fees have been rising this year, albeit at a slower pace than last year, and Canadian mortgage rates remain fairly high, despite the Bank of Canada's recent rate cuts. For these reasons, I thought that TD had a decent shot at beating on earnings.

Despite this, I was still surprised by the strength of the results that TD actually put out. I figured that the U.S. retail segment would hold overall results back enough that growth would be pretty minimal. Instead, TD actually delivered its best growth rates in many quarters. The bank also pledged to continue its ongoing buyback program, which it initiated after selling off its Charles Schwab (SCHW) stake to comply with the asset cap that the Department of Justice ((DoJ)) imposed on it late last year.

When I last covered TD Bank, I rated the stock a buy on the grounds that it was making heady progress in recovering from its anti-money laundering (AML) scandal and associated penalties. Since then, the bank has gone on to perform even better than I expected--and my expectations were pretty high. While TD stock is up 13% since my article published, I'm actually even more bullish on it now than I was then, because of the company's strong earnings performance. Accordingly, I am upgrading my TD rating to strong buy.

Earnings Recap

TD Bank's second quarter earnings release was quite strong, boasting the following metrics:

  • Revenue: C$15.14 billion, up 9.1% year over year (beat by C$1.7 billion).
  • Net interest income (NII): $8.125 billion, up 8.8%.
  • Reported EPS: C$6.28, up 305%.
  • Adjusted EPS: C$1.97, down 2.4% (beat by $0.19).
  • A 9 million share reduction.
  • A 14.9 CET1 ratio.
  • A 12.4% return on equity.
  • A 15% return on tangible equity.
  • A 4.7 leverage ratio.

For the most part, the metrics that TD announced in its second quarter earnings release were outstanding. The company grew its revenue, NII and reported earnings; realized a nice one-time gain on its Schwab stake; and also delivered high capital adequacy and liquidity.

One thing definitely wasn't so hot in TD's second quarter release, though: the U.S. retail segment's performance. The segment's net income was $120 million, down 76%. That was not such a great showing, although nobody was really expecting U.S. retail to grow--the $430 billion asset cap threw cold water on any possibility of that happening.

The Canadian retail segment's showing was mixed, with revenue up 3% but earnings down 4%

Wealth Management and Insurance did well, with revenue up 12% and earnings up 14%.

Last but not least, wholesale banking (essentially investment banking) achieved solid results, with revenue up 10%, reported earnings up 16% and adjusted earnings up 1%. The investment bank's showing was particularly impressive considering that Donald Trump's on-and-off tariffs have been having a chilling effect on M&A activity.

Future Prospects

Having explored TD's recent quarterly results, we can now turn to evaluating its future prospects. For this, we need to look at the earnings call transcript.

CEO Raymond Chun said on TD's earnings call that forecasting future results would be difficult because of the macroeconomic uncertainty Donald Trump's tariff policies created. He noted the U.S.-China tariff deal as a positive, but also said that U.S.-Canada trade disagreements remained. U.S. operations head Leo Salom said that the company had been making progress with its AML remediation program, drawing on AI and other advanced technologies to identify crimes like money laundering before they occur. These measures increase costs but decrease the probability of TD taking another AML fine; on balance they will likely have a positive effect on TD's future earnings.

The only specific numerical guidance that TD gave on its earnings call was for NII, which it said that it expected to increase by C$300 million to C$500 million for the year. Assuming that tax rates and other such items don't change, then, TD's earnings will probably go up by at least C$300 million. In fact, they will likely go up more, as earnings in 2024's fourth quarter were impacted by a C$3 billion fine. The lack of a fine in 2025's fourth quarter will presumably have a positive effect on earnings growth for the whole year. For this reason, I would expect TD's earnings for 2025 to be equivalent to last year's plus C$3.5 billion, so roughly C$12.3 billion, or C$7 per share on an adjusted basis.

Valuation

Having explored TD's recent earnings and future prospects, we can now proceed to valuing the shares. We can start with the multiples that Seeking Alpha Quant has on file.

As you can see, TD scores a respectable 'B' on valuation in Seeking Alpha's quant rankings. Some standout metrics include the 9.6 GAAP P/E ratio and the 0.15 PEG ratio. These are cheaper than what is typical for North American money center banks today.

On the other hand, I estimated earlier that TD would earn C$7.06 in earnings per share for 2025. The TSX-listed version of TD trades at C$92.91, so that's a 13.16 P/E ratio if TD earns what I expect it to. This is higher than the multiples Seeking Alpha Quant has on file. However, it's still relatively low.

What kind of multiple does TD deserve to trade at? We can approach that with discounted cash flows. The 10-year treasury currently has a 4.5% yield. Discounting my $7.06 EPS estimate of 4.5% produces a C$155 price target, or $113 for the U.S. listed shares.

Now, TD is not risk-free, so we should probably throw in a risk premium here. Using a 3% risk premium (a 7.5% discount rate), TD is worth C$94 or $68.71. There remains only a small amount of upside here. However, this is all based on a no-growth assumption. I think TD will probably continue growing long term, and that the growth will make it worth more than what the calculations above indicate.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on TD's second quarter earnings release is that it exceeded expectations on every metric. Not only did the bank beat estimates for the quarter just reported, its executives also said that they expect positive growth in NII for the current fiscal year. Based on this, I'm expecting good things from TD in the year ahead. So, I rate the stock a strong buy.

data.title: Golar LNG Partners Preferred Units: Distribution Might Be Gone Forever
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Golar LNG Partners (OTC:GMLPF) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

Four months ago, I raised my rating on Golar LNG Partners' 8.75% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Preferred Units ("the Preferred Units") from "Sell" to "Hold" after struggling parent New Fortress Energy (NFE) managed to raise additional capital and refinance some near-term debt maturities.

As a reminder:

Four years ago, New Fortress Energy acquired Golar LNG Partners in a $1.9 billion transaction and subsequently delisted the partnership's Preferred Units from Nasdaq.

With the Preferred Units still trading on the OTC Expert Market as of today, most retail investors remain precluded from transactions in the securities except for closing existing positions. However, a small number of brokers still seem to permit opening new positions. In case of doubt, investors should contact their broker on this topic.

In recent years, debt-laden parent New Fortress Energy has stripped the company of its assets and left the partnership as an empty shell without sufficient funds to cover approximately $3 million in regular quarterly cash distributions to holders of the company's Preferred Units.

But until recently, the partnership had not missed a single quarterly interest payment thanks to an "irrevocable letter of support" provided by parent New Fortress Energy as outlined in Golar LNG Partners' 2023 financial statements on page F-10 (emphasis added by author):

The ability of the Partnership to continue as a going concern is dependent upon the continued financial support from the ultimate parent undertaking, NFE.

As of April 29, 2024, the issuance date of these consolidated financial statements, the Partnership concluded that it is probable that its continued funding from NFE will be sufficient to fund dividends on the preferred stock, operating expenses, financial commitments, and other cash requirements for at least one year after the issuance date of these financial statements. NFE also provided an irrevocable letter of support whereby NFE has committed to fund such commitments.

Unfortunately, my upgrade turned out to be premature, as New Fortress Energy's financial woes have continued in recent months.

Last week, the company reported abysmal first quarter results and guided core 2025 Adjusted EBITDA well below expectations, which caused a massive selloff in New Fortress Energy's common shares.

Not surprisingly, Golar LNG Partners' Preferred Units were hit hard, too:

With New Fortress Energy's debt also trading at deeply distressed levels, odds of a near-term debt restructuring have increased:

With more than $5 billion in senior secured debt at the holding level as of the end of last year, any sort of recovery for unsecured creditors and equity holders would be highly unlikely.

In fact, even secured lenders are preparing for a major haircut as evidenced by the company's bond prices.

Please note also that New Fortress Energy has failed to file its quarterly report on form 10-Q in a timely manner which constitutes a violation of credit facility covenants.

Given the parent's escalating debt issues, I don't consider it a surprise that Golar LNG Partners has not paid the quarterly cash distribution on the scheduled payment date:

While distributions are cumulative, the partnership remains entirely dependent on support from ailing parent New Fortress Energy.

Depending on the terms of the parent's irrevocable letter of support, preferred unitholders might be permitted an unsecured claim in a potential New Fortress Energy bankruptcy.

While there might be lawsuits pertaining to the sale of the company's assets and subsequent distribution of the proceeds to parent New Fortress Energy in recent years, the most likely outcome would also be an unsecured claim.

However, with unsecured creditors likely to end up with nothing in this case, odds for a recovery seem firmly stacked against preferred unitholders.

But even if New Fortress Energy will somehow escape bankruptcy, the distribution is not likely to be reinstated anytime soon, if ever.

The issue is well-reflected in the current unit price, which represents a 93% discount to the preferred unit's stated liquidation value of $25:

With the common units apparently being a hopeless case, a downgrade to "Sell" or even "Strong Sell" seems appropriate.

However, with the vast majority of the damage already done, I prefer to wait for disclosures in Golar LNG Partners' eagerly awaited annual report and New Fortress Energy's delayed quarterly report on form 10-Q before making changes to my "Hold" rating.

Bottom Line

Time to apologize for a premature upgrade in January, as Golar LNG Partners has neither declared nor paid its quarterly preferred unit distribution of $0.546875 per unit for the period from February 15, 2025 to May 14, 2025.

However, given parent New Fortress Energy's escalating debt issues, the non-payment can be hardly considered a surprise as the partnership remains an empty shell and as such requires persistent financial support in order to make preferred unit distributions.

For my part, I expect New Fortress Energy to restructure its debt obligations in bankruptcy with secured creditors becoming the new owners of the business while unsecured creditors and equity holders are likely to end up holding the bag.

Even in case the parent manages to somehow escape chapter 11, Golar LNG Partners' preferred unit distribution is not likely to be reinstated anytime soon if ever.

With most of the damage already done, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating until more tangible information transpires.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ANZ Group: The Cheapest Of Australia's Big Four Banks
data.description:
Margin pressure from a ferocious retail banking market and costly technology spending is not making life easy for Australia's ANZ Group (OTCPK:ANZGY)(OTCPK:ANZGF). Not only has that lumbered ANZ with stagnant earnings since my last article in 2022, but it has also bled through to its shares. While a ~29% total return isn't bad in that time (it's equal to a little over 10% annualized), this still lands ANZ near the bottom of the pack versus peers.

That said, I do see reasons for optimism. For one, ANZ should be able to realize some longer-term margin relief as its digital initiatives lead to efficiencies in legacy parts of the business. More important than that, though, ANZ's underperformance has left it the cheapest of the Big Four, and by some distance to boot. With that mapping to a 5.7% dividend yield (and a bit higher including buybacks), ANZ would be my current pick for investors seeking exposure to Australian banks.

Margin Pressure

On the surface, ANZ hasn't fared all that badly in the two-and-a-half years since my last piece. While the Reserve Bank of Australia has cut around 50bps from the peak, the cash rate is still around 100bps higher than it was back then, and this ought to be good for a bank with a high stock of variable rate loans operating in a deposit market as concentrated as Australia.

At first blush, this does appear to be the case. ANZ made A$3.57 billion in cash profit over the first half of FY2025 (its fiscal year ends in September), around 15% higher than the equivalent period in 2022. However, around 60% of this increase comes from last year's acquisition of smaller Australian bank Suncorp. Excluding this, ANZ's legacy business has only seen earnings grow by around A$175 million in that time.

A couple of things in particular appear to have been weighing on the bank. For one, interest margins have contracted, particularly in its Australian retail business, which accounted for around 20% of ANZ's total cash profit in H1. When I last covered it, ANZ's net interest margin in this segment was 2.29%. This has fallen to 1.84% as of H1 2025.

Some of this is due to generic late cycle behavior. For example, as the higher interest rate environment wore on, bank's like ANZ have had to pass on more of the rise in rates to their own depositors. The deposit mix has also shifted toward more expensive Term accounts.

There have also been headwinds on the asset side, chiefly in residential mortgages. Not only is this market ferociously competitive, but ANZ has also been targeting a large slice of the pie, with its share of Australian home loans increasing by around 40bps since my last update. Achieving this will have required ANZ to be somewhat more aggressive on pricing. While interest margins have been much more resilient in its other divisions (Australia Commercial, New Zealand, et al.), ANZ's overall NIM has fallen from 1.68% to 1.56% since my last update.

ANZ has also seen pressure from the expenses line, specifically technology costs. This is partly due to its ongoing rollout of digital retail offering ANZ Plus, which now has over a million customers and A$20 billion in customer deposits (both up from virtually zero when I last covered it).

The plan is to run the entire Australian retail bank from ANZ Plus by the end of the decade, and this will eventually allow ANZ to save money by retiring legacy platforms. In the meantime, though, this is costing dearly it in terms of efficiency.

As a result of these issues, ANZ's cost-to-income ratio has increased from 48.8% to 62.3% in its Australian retail business since my last update (higher obviously being worse for this metric), while its consolidated C/I ratio has expanded by around 330bps to 52.1%. For reference, best-in-class peer Commonwealth Bank of Australia (OTCPK:CMWAY)(OTCPK:CBAUF) has kept its retail C/I ratio virtually flat in that time at around 39.5%.

Grounds For Optimism

While ANZ has had its issues, I still like the stock for two reasons. Firstly, some of its margin pressure should be self-correcting once ANZ Plus reaches scale in a few years. Per above, there was only a ten point gap between ANZ and Commonwealth in terms of their respective C/I ratios back in 2022. Over time, I expect this gap to narrow once again as ANZ realizes some low-hanging efficiency gains. That, in turn, should be a tailwind for earnings growth.

More important than that, ANZ's underperformance has left it the cheapest of the Big Four. At A$29.07, the stock currently trades for a multiple of 1.31x net tangible assets (A$22.14 per share), roughly 40bps-300bps cheaper than peers depending on the name. Some of that can be explained by ANZ's relative weaker return on equity, but only some. Indeed, on a P/E basis ANZ's 13.1x multiple (based on trailing-twelve-month cash EPS) also represents a clear discount to peers, as shown below.

The above also maps to decent capital returns potential. Being a large bank in a mature concentrated market, ANZ's organic growth prospects are relatively limited. That warrants a high dividend payout ratio. On that basis, the current annualized dividend of A$1.66 per share seems reasonable, with that equating to around 75% of TTM net income. The yield is a decent-looking 5.7%, which is around 80bps-300bps higher than its Big Four peers.

This also opens up some room for buybacks. Indeed, ANZ has a AUD 2 billion program in play from 2024. That equates to an additional ~2.3% yield. Since ANZ's Common Equity Tier 1 capital ratio is a healthy 11.8%, I view that as reasonable.

Conclusion

ANZ Group has certainly been one of the weaker performers among the Big Four Australian banks since I last covered it, with margin pressure going some way to explaining this. Given its tech spending, I do think some of that pressure will naturally fade in the years ahead, as ANZ Plus eventually allows ANZ to realize efficiency gains from legacy platforms. More importantly, ANZ's struggles have left it trading at a clear discount to peers in terms of P/NTA, P/E and dividend yield. As such, it would be my pick for investors seeking exposure to the Australian banking space.

We are evaluating opportunities in companies that are relatively insulated from the potential impact of tariffs, such as those with sales driven primarily by domestic demand.

Fund strategy

  • Uses a consistent process to offer a diversified portfolio of foreign small- and mid-cap companies with strong growth potential.
  • Invests in quality stocks at reasonable valuations — innovative companies with favorable market structure and high barriers to entry.
  • Benefits from a team of analysts, specializing in small- and mid-cap investing and specialized by industry or geographic region, who use a bottom-up approach to stock selection.

Fund performance

  • Columbia Acorn International Institutional Share Class (MUTF:ACINX) returned - 1.43% for the three months ending March 31, 2025.
  • The fund's benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Small Mid Cap Growth Index, returned 2.44% for the same period.
  • In sector terms, positioning in financials, communication services and consumer discretionary led positive contributions to performance relative to the benchmark. Selection within industrials was the biggest detractor by a wide margin.

Market overview

Uncertainty around the scope of impending U.S. tariffs on its trading partners weighed on global equity markets in the first quarter. In addition, many information technology stocks sold off on the release of an artificial intelligence model by Chinese company DEEPSEEK. The model was developed at a claimed fraction of the cost of OpenAl's Chat GPT AI assistant, leading investors to question projected spending levels on AI datacenters. Conversely, European equity markets rose with defense-oriented stocks leading the way as Germany's parliament repealed the nation's "debt brake," thereby paving the way for massive spending on infrastructure and defense. In Japan, returns were muted, as the central bank embarked on a rate-hiking cycle. China was the standout performer among major markets, with double-digit equity returns propelled by government stimulus and enthusiasm around AI in the wake of the DeepSeek announcement. In a reversal of longstanding trends, international stocks outpaced U.S. stocks, and value stocks notably outperformed their growth counterparts.

Quarterly portfolio recap

In sector terms, positioning in financials, communication services and consumer discretionary led positive contributions to performance relative to the benchmark. Selection within industrials was the biggest detractor by a wide margin.

In broad terms, the bulk of the fund's underperformance was driven by our exposure within industrials and IT to stocks that suffered from the scaling back of expectations for AI spending, as well as by a lack of exposure to defense stocks within industrials.

Looking at individual positions, positive contributions to the fund's performance relative to the benchmark were led by Bank of Ireland, Ireland's largest bank in terms of assets and the second largest by market share. The stock carries an attractive relative valuation and was boosted by the bank's late-February announcement of a significant share repurchase program.

Simplex Holdings is a Japanese IT consulting firm providing systems strategy, development and maintenance, with a clientele represented by a broad array of Japanese financial institutions. The stock rose, as the company posted encouraging quarterly results, driven by a rebound in consultant utilization and effective cost control efforts.

Convatec Group is a provider of wound care, infusion and ostomy care products. The company has a strong focus on research and development, which has enabled it to distinguish itself from competitors through the ease of use of its products. The company provided a positive update on its medium-term growth and margin outlook, while outlining plans to optimize its product portfolio through further innovation and an increased focus on chronic care relative to acute care products.

On the downside, within industrials, we saw a negative impact on holdings that are exposed to datacenters, most notably Munters Group, a Swedish manufacturer of dehumidification, cooling and air quality systems. Munters sells into a wide range of end markets including the automotive, agriculture, construction and health care industries, to name only a few. In recent quarters, the company experienced strong order flow growth driven by its datacenter cooling solutions, and sentiment around the stock suffered from the more clouded outlook for that segment.

Recruit Holdings is the Japan-based operator of job-matching engine Indeed. The company has meaningful U.S. exposure, and the cuts to public sector jobs initiated by the new Department of Government Efficiency weighed on the stock, which had been a strong performer in 2024.

An off-benchmark position in Vietnamese information consultant FPT Corp. was another laggard in the quarter. The company experienced delays in rolling out a planned Al factory to support cloud services for its clients and accelerate its development of AI platforms and applications. We exited the position in the quarter.

Japan-based Disco Corp. is a precision tool maker with a strong market position in providing cutting and grinding equipment to the semiconductor industry. Disco has been an AI beneficiary, as its tools are heavily utilized in the advanced packaging processes used to manufacture high bandwidth memory and high-end graphical processing units (GPUs). The company experienced a slowing of shipments beginning in late 2024, driven, in part, by a pause in spending on datacenter expansion. We believe Disco is positioned to benefit over the remainder of 2025 as production of the next generation of HBM ramps up and Nvidia (NVDA) releases its new GPU architecture.

Outlook

The fund is positioned with a somewhat conservative tilt in view of the heightened uncertainty around global trade and the broader economic trajectory, with a focus on companies with stable business models. In addition, we are evaluating opportunities in companies that are relatively insulated from the potential impact of tariffs, such as those with sales driven primarily by domestic demand.

That said, we are cognizant of the potential for headlines to send stocks sharply higher as we have seen with announcements of shifting U.S. trade policies. In addition to any positive news on trade, any move by the U.S. Federal Reserve to lower interest rates would likely be well received by the market and benefit sentiment around growth oriented stocks. Given these variables, while the fund is defensive on the margins, we are taking care to maintain a balanced exposure to the varying risks and possible macroeconomic outcomes.

data.title: Parsons' Growth Story Falls Short When Compared To Peers
data.description:
Investment thesis

Parsons Corporation (NYSE:PSN) has delivered strong top-line growth over the past 8 years. But this was driven by acquisitions. However, this growth has not translated into superior returns compared to its peers. It ranked near the bottom among peers in EBIT margin, return on capital, and free cash flow. Despite improved cost control, I have concerns about its unsustainable reinvestment rate. My valuation also did not provide any margin of safety. As such, Parsons is not a compelling value opportunity.

Business background

Parsons is a consulting company and an engineering contractor, but with a strong tilt toward technology-driven defense, intelligence, and infrastructure solutions. Its business is organized into two main segments:

  • Federal Solutions. This segment delivers high-end technology and mission-critical services primarily to US government agencies.
  • Critical Infrastructure. This segment focuses on modernizing physical and digital infrastructure.

The company IPOed in 2019. In the initial years of its public-listed life, the Critical Infrastructure segment was the biggest revenue contributor. However, by 2024, the Federal Solutions segment had overtaken it. Refer to the left part of Chart 1.

With the Federal Solutions segment as an important revenue contributor, you should not be surprised that North America accounted for the bulk of the company’s total revenue. Refer to the right part of Chart 1.

Looking at its business profile, I would describe Parsons as serving the US defense and global civil engineering markets. These are single-digit growth markets:

“The US Defense Market Size is estimated at USD 320.86 billion in 2025…at a CAGR of 3.58% during the forecast period (2025 – 2030)” Mordor Intelligence.

“The global Civil Engineering Market is…Valued at USD 9,162.7 million in 2023… growing at a 5.3% CAGR from 2024 to 2033.” Market.US

Acquisition-driven revenue growth

While serving low-single-digit markets, Parsons has been able to deliver revenue growth of 12.2% CAGR from 2017 to 2024. A big part of this was due to the more than a dozen acquisitions it has undertaken since 2018. These have enabled it to leapfrog into high-tech verticals, especially for the US defense and intelligence communities.

To give you a sense of the acquisitions, from 2017 to 2025, Parsons spent about USD 2.5 billion on acquisitions compared to USD 0.3 billion on CAPEX.

Looking at Chart 1, you can see that the revenues for 2023 and 2024 were substantially higher than those pre-2023. Revenue growth rates were 30% and 24% for 2023 and 2024, respectively. The company attributed these to the high organic growth rates.

“The increase in Federal Solutions revenue for… 2023…was primarily due to organic growth of 25%...The increase in Critical Infrastructure revenue for…2023…was substantially due to organic growth.” 2023 Form 10K

“The increase in Federal Solutions revenue for… 2024…was primarily related to organic growth of 30%...The increase in Critical Infrastructure revenue for… 2024 was primarily related to organic growth of 12%...” 2024 Form 10K.

Parsons' high organic growth in 2023–2024 was driven by a unique convergence of contract timing, government stimulus, and strong execution. As mentioned earlier, Parsons operates in single-digit growth sectors.

As such, I would not expect the double-digit revenue growth rate due to organic growth to continue. In this context, the company, in its Q1 2025 Presentation, provided guidance of 5% for organic growth.

Moving forward, I would expect the company to continue with its acquisition-led growth path. The company alluded to this in its Q1 2025 presentation.

"Parsons to continue to make internal investments and accretive acquisitions to drive additional growth.”

Growth needs to be funded on one metric for this is the Reinvestment.

Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisition – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital

From 2017 to 2022, Parsons had an average Reinvestment rate (Reinvestment/NOPAT) of 181%. This is an unsustainable rate. Excluding the acquisitions, the average Reinvestment rate was negative. The negative rate was because the Depreciation & Amortization more than offset the CAPEX for many years.

Neither the greater than 100% nor the negative rates are sustainable rates. Over the long run, I would expect the Reinvestment rate to be governed by the fundamental growth equation.

Growth = Return X Reinvestment rate.

Based on the past 2 years' average Return (ROIC) of 10% and the projected 7% 2025 growth rate, I would expect the Reinvestment rate to go down to below 70%.

Improving operating trends

While revenue grew at 12.2% from 2017 to 2018, PAT grew at 14.7% CAGR. This slightly better profit growth rate was due more to higher revenue and better cost control rather than margin expansion.

You can see from the right part of Chart 2 that while there was some uptrend in the gross profit margin, it peaked in 2021 and trended down thereafter. This is reflected in the contribution margin trend. Refer to the left part of Chart 2.

I think that 2021 seemed to be an outlier, as exemplified by the following quotes from the 2021 and 2024 Forms 10k:

“…27.8% of our operating income during fiscal 2021… was derived from equity in our unconsolidated joint ventures.”

“…5.5% of our operating income during fiscal 2024, 16.6%...during fiscal 2023, and 8.8%... during fiscal 2022 was derived from equity in our unconsolidated joint ventures…”

However, the Selling, General and Administration (SGA) margins seemed to have a stronger downtrend post-2021. This is reflected by the declining fixed cost margin that averaged 17% for 2023/24 compared to 18% for 2017/18. In other words, fixed costs did not grow as fast as revenue.

Notes to Chart 2:

I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA and Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

The better profit performance translated into better returns.

  • ROIC averaged 10% for 2023/24 compared to the average 4% for the first 2 years of the IPO (2019/20).
  • ROE averaged 10% for 2023/24 compared to the 7% average for 2019/20.

These past 2 years' returns far exceeded their cost of funds, suggesting value was created for the shareholders as the company grew bigger.

Peer comparison

I compared Parsons' performance with the following listed companies that served similar markets:

  • AECOM (ACM) This is a global infrastructure consulting firm that delivers architecture, engineering, and project management services for transportation, environmental, and urban development projects worldwide.
  • Tetra Tech (TTEK). Tetra Tech is a leading provider of high-end consulting and engineering services focused on water, environment, sustainable infrastructure, and energy management.
  • CACI International (CACI). CACI delivers IT modernization, cybersecurity, and advanced intelligence solutions to US defense, intelligence, and federal civilian agencies.
  • Leidos Holdings (LDOS). Leidos is a science and technology company providing mission-critical solutions in defense, healthcare, civil infrastructure, and cybersecurity for the US government.

Parsons is a mid-sized player among its peers in terms of 2024 revenue, but ranked No. 1 in terms of growth rate. Refer to Table 1.

You can see that despite operating in single-digit growth sectors, most of the peers delivered double-digit growth rates. Note that TTEK, CACI and LDOS growths were also driven by acquisitions.

I compared Parsons' performance with that of its peers based on the following metrics. Refer to Charts 3 and 4. Overall, I would rank Parsons’ performance as below average.

  • Parsons ranked at the bottom for the return on capital.
  • While Parsons' EBIT margin has been trending up, so have those of the peers. As such, Parsons continued to deliver one of the worst EBIT margins.
  • I would also rate Parsons' unlevered free cash flow margin as one of the worst.
  • In terms of quantum, I would consider Parsons EPS as average. But it ranked No. 4 in terms of EPS growth rate.

Financial Position

I would consider Parsons as moderately sound financially, as its positive points offset its negative concerns. Its positive points are:

As of the end Dec. 2024, it had USD 453 million in cash, equal to about 8% of its total assets.

Over the past 8 years, it generated positive cash flow from operations every year. During this period, it had a good cash flow conversion rate, generating a total of USD 2.4 billion in cash flow from operations compared to the total PAT of 1.3 billion.

My concerns were:

It did not have a good capital allocation plan, as shown in Table 2. You can see that the cash flow from operations was not enough to fund its CAPEX and acquisitions. It has to raise additional capital.

It has a DE ratio of 56% as of the end of Dec 2024, the highest post-IPO.

It had an unsustainable Reinvestment rate. The key issue was the aggressive acquisitions and the funding for this. But this is within management control and can be brought to a sustainable Reinvestment rate.

Valuation

My analysis showed that Parsons has reached a size where it can deliver value, creating growth and profits. As such, I adopted the following picture in valuing Parsons.

  • The base revenue would be the 2025 revenue growing at 7% in Year 1. I adopted the 2025 management guidance for this. The growth rate would reduce proportionately to the 4% terminal growth rate in Year 6.
  • The base contribution margin would be the average 2017 to 2024 values and remain unchanged during the valuation period.
  • The base capita efficiency would be the average 2023 to 2024 values and remain unchanged during the valuation period.
  • The base Reinvestment rate would be the average 2023 to 2024 values and reduced proportionately to one based on the fundamental growth equation in the terminal year.
  • The fixed cost would be pegged to the average 2023 to 2024 fixed cost margin to reflect the cost improvements achieved.
  • The tax rate would be the post-IPO average tax rate of 23% (excluding the tax credit of 2019).

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 55 per share compared to its market price (21 May 2025) of USD 67 per share. There is no margin of safety.

Valuation model

I valued Parsons based on a multi-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) model, as shown in Table 3.

The basic equations used in the model are:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) – Reinvestment.

EBIT = Revenue X Contribution margin – Fixed cost

The contribution margin and fixed cost margin are based on the operating model shown in the right part of Chart 2.

The Reinvestment was based on the Reinvestment margin.

The fixed cost was based on the fixed cost margin.

Notes to Table 3:

a. Proportionate reduction

b. Pegged to the growth rate

c. Assumed no improvement

d. Revenue X fixed cost margin

e. Revenue X Margin, and after accounting for Fixed costs

f. Assumed proportionate improvement

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Assumed constant D/E ratio. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

l. Terminal for the year discounted at a terminal growth rate

m. 5 years NPV + terminal value

n. Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI, and Debt

o. Based on the number of shares

The WACC used in the valuation model was based on the first page results of a Google search for “Parsons WACC”. Refer to Table 4.

Risks and limitations

My valuation was based on a conservative revenue growth assumption. I assumed that revenue growth for Year 1 would be 7% as per the Q1 2025 guidance and would be reduced proportionately to the terminal growth rate of 4% in Year 6. This would result in a CAGR of 5.5% over the valuation period. This is much lower than the historical growth rate of 12.2% CAGR.

If I assumed that the average growth rate for the valuation period was the historical CAGR, I would have an intrinsic value of USD 97 per share. Note that this value assumes that the Reinvestment rate in the terminal year reduces to one given by the growth equation.

I will leave it to you to decide whether it makes sense to challenge management guidance on the 2025 revenue growth rate.

But I think the biggest concern is how fast management can bring down the Reinvestment rate to one given by the growth equation. In practice, this would mean having a more cost-effective acquisition program. This could come from paying less and/or extracting more synergies from the acquisitions.

In this context, I worry about the effectiveness of the acquisitions.

  • From 2017 to 2024, the company spent USD 2.5 billion on acquisitions.
  • During the period, PAT went from USD 111 million in 2017 to USD 291 million in 2025.
  • Assuming all the profits came from the acquisition, there was a USD 180 million growth in profits.
  • Assuming this figure annually, we have a 14-year payback period.

I know this is a rough calculation, but it does point to a long payback period. This implies some overpaying and/or not enough synergy. For a company whose growth is dependent on acquisitions, this raises a big red flag.

Conclusion

Over the past 8 years, Parsons has delivered top-line and bottom-line growth. But revenue growth was driven more by acquisitions rather than organic growth. Profit growth was due more to higher revenue and better fixed cost control rather than margin expansion.

At the same time, its overall financial performance remains below average compared to its peers. Return on capital continues to lag, and despite an improving EBIT margin trend, the company still ranks near the bottom in absolute margin levels. The unlevered free cash flow margin is similarly weak, reflecting high reinvestment demands.

In short, while Parsons has scale and growth momentum, its capital efficiency and profitability metrics suggest it still has ground to cover to match best-in-class performance in its sector.

You can understand why I would only rate Parsons as moderately sound from a fundamental perspective. At the same time, my valuation did not provide any margin of safety. This is not an investment from a long-term value investment perspective.

data.title: Stocks Take Another Tariff To The Shin
data.description:
Markets sagged again Friday after a fresh round of tariff threats landed like a sandbag, flattening retailers still wheezing from the last batch. Stocks clawed back from session lows, but still logged a 2% weekly drop. This trade war is the market’s Newtonian nemesis - what goes up meets tariffs and comes down.

Level Change 5/23/25 (%)

-0.6 Dow

-1.0 Nasdaq

-0.9 Nasdaq 100

-0.7 S&P 500

-0.2 S&P 400

-0.6 S&P 600

Trade returned to the front burner - and promptly boiled over. President Trump declared that talks with the EU are “going nowhere” and vowed a “straight 50% tariff” on all EU goods starting June 1. That’s more than twice the 20% bluff, quintuple the 10% goodwill pause, and roughly par for the posturing.

This followed reports that the US would reject the EU’s latest offer on a call with Trade Rep Jamieson Greer. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent, reprising his role as tariff translator, said countries must “negotiate in good faith or face the US simply setting a tariff rate.” It’s trade policy by way of “clean your room, or I’ll do it for you.”

The EU didn’t flinch. Trade Commissioner Maroš Šefčovič insisted that “mutual respect, not threats,” should guide policy, and pledged to defend EU interests. Analysts pointed out the White House vow wasn’t an executive order (yet) and called it a classic Trump tactic: threaten big, negotiate later.

Apple (AAPL -3%) took its turn in the barrel after Trump warned that iPhones must be made in the US or face a 25% tariff - “at least.” Precision vagueness aside, Wedbush dismissed the idea as “a fairy tale.” Tripling the iPhone’s price just to stamp “Made in America” on the back might rupture even Cupertino’s famed reality-distortion field.

Of course, there’s a reason iPhones aren’t made in America. Apple spent billions training engineers abroad, where China and India offer deeper talent pools at a fraction of US wages. Steve Jobs put it bluntly to President Obama in 2010: Apple needed 30,000 industrial engineers. “You can’t find that many in America to hire,” he said. “If you could educate these engineers, we could move more manufacturing plants here.”

Fifteen years later, the gap remains, and Trump’s tariff-driven wish won’t close it overnight. Attention spans matter, especially when paired with a long-term plan. Just ask China.

Retailers didn’t wait for tariffs to wreck margins - they jumped first. Ross Stores (ROST -9.9%) tanked after yanking its full-year outlook, blaming “evolving trade policies” and stubborn inflation. Deckers Outdoor (DECK -19.9%) fell even harder, warning of weakened demand and tariff tolls on its Hoka and Ugg brands. When shoes drop this hard, they leave a crater.

The Fed hasn’t exactly broken into song, either.

Chicago’s Austan Goolsbee said the economy is holding up for now but warned that tariff-driven stagflation is “the central bank’s worst situation.” He’s still hopeful the US can return to the solid growth it enjoyed before the April 2 “Liberation Day” shock, and suggested interest rates might be “a fair bit below where they are today” in 10 to 16 months. With luck, that’s enough time for the baby parade to march out of earshot.

And that was Friday: fresh tariff threats, falling shoes, and a president confident that wishful thinking can outpace engineering shortages.
