GLD, IAU, and U.S. credit rating

I last wrote on the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:GLD) on April 11. That article was titled "GLD Vs. SLV: Higher Recession Probability Makes GLD More Timely." As hinted in the title, the analysis is more oriented toward the near term. It rated GLD as a strong buy due to its role as hedging against the following recession risks at that time:

Recent changes in geopolitics have increased the probability of a recession. This leads me to see a better return/risk ratio for GLD than SLV. I expect GLD to demonstrate better resilience in market downturns both in terms of lower drawdowns and quicker recovery time. In contrast, SLV has more exposure to industrial applications, which tends to suffer negative impacts in the case of a recession.

As for the iShares Gold Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:IAU), I last wrote on it back in March in an article titled "GDX Vs. IAU: Why I Prefer IAU For Our Gold Exposure." That analysis is also geared toward the near term. It rated IAU as a buy because I saw it as a better way to capitalize on the gold price rally than gold miners.

Since those articles, there have been several changes in our macroeconomic parameters. The most consequential one to me was the recent downgrade of U.S. government credit ratings. In case you're unfamiliar with this development, the following news provides the background needed for the rest of this article. The downgrade immediately caused reaction in both the bonds and currency markets. Combined with other developments since my last writing (mostly the tariff drama), the dollar index (i.e., the ICE US Dollar Index, DXY) has declined substantially in the past month or so, as you can see from the following chart. The ICE US Dollar Index currently stands at 99.88, only slightly above the average level of 98.40 in the past 10 years.

On May 16, Moody’s Ratings downgraded the U.S. credit rating, citing an inability of the nation to address large and growing deficits. This downgrade means that for the first time ever, all three major credit ratings agencies have downgraded U.S. credit below their top rating. Moody’s warning comes as lawmakers are considering budget reconciliation legislation that would make America’s fiscal situation significantly worse.

These changes have promoted me to rethink my gold thesis for the longer term, and my thesis in this article are twofold. First, I expect the impacts for the U.S. credit downgrade to be long-lasting and to keep pressuring the dollar index. As gold is priced in dollars, this leads me to feel bullish about both IAU and GLD (and other similar gold ETFs). Second, for long-term holdings, I prefer IAU over GLD due to its lower fees. These considerations have led me to adjust my rating on GLD to buy and my rating on IAU to strong buy.

GLD and IAU ETFs: Basic facts

Before going into further details, let’s start with a quick intro for GLD and IAU in case there are readers new to them. Both funds are categorized as commodity funds as they're backed by physical gold bullion as detailed in their fund descriptions below.

GLD Description: GLD tracks the gold spot price, less expenses and liabilities, using gold bars held in London vaults. GLD is the first to market to invest directly in physical gold. Actively traded, the shares provide deep liquidity. NAV for the fund is determined using the LBMA PM Gold Price (formerly the London PM Gold Fix), so GLD has an extremely close relationship with spot prices.

IAU Description: IAU tracks the gold spot price, less expenses and liabilities, using gold bars held in vaults around the world. IAU is an efficient way for investors to hold physical gold. The fund removes the complexities for retail investors of buying, transporting, storing and insuring physical gold. Using a grantor trust structure, the fund is a very stable fund.

To my knowledge, GLD and IAU are two of the largest and oldest gold ETFs with GLD incepted in 2004 and IAU a year later in 2005. GLD manages a large AUM of $97.52 billion, and IAU's AUM is also sizable at $45.93 billion. A notable difference lies in their expense ratios, with GLD having a higher ratio of 0.40% compared to IAU's 0.25%, and I will further elaborate on this difference later.

In recent years, a few new competing ETFs have been issued that could offer more attractive alternatives. For example, GLDM has accumulated an AUM of $14.48 billion and charges an expense ratio of 0.10%, offering an attractive combination of liquidity and fees when compared to GLD and IAU. I will say more about these alternatives later in this article.

GLD and IAU: What history taught me

With the above introduction, now let me get back to my thesis and explain why I expect the U.S. credit downgrade to generate long-lasting impacts. To properly contextualize things, the chart below shows how rare such downgrades have been historically. Moody's has maintained its Aaa rating for the United States throughout the ups and downs in the past. I do see good reasons for Moody's downgrade at this junction, given the uncertainties of the budget reconciliation.

These uncertainties could cause a range of potential long-term impacts, including the loss of investor confidence, the reduced demand for U.S. assets, and also higher borrowing costs for the U.S. government. But the impact that I fear the most is the erosion of U.S. dollar’s global reserve status. While immediate and drastic changes are unlikely, a credit rating downgrade could weaken the dollar's status as the world's primary reserve currency over the long term. This could cause other countries to gradually diversify away from dollar-denominated reserves.

Both IAU and GLD could help investors hedge this long-term effect in at least two ways. First, if/when dollar’s status as a reserve currency weakens, I expect it to reduce global demand for the dollar, causing a sustained decline. As aforementioned, gold is priced in dollars (at least for the foreseeable future), a decline in dollar index tends to boost gold prices. Second, if/when other countries seek to diversify their reserve, I expect gold to be a top candidate in their diversification.

Of course, I do not expect these above considerations to play out in the near term – and I’ve warned you that this article is more oriented toward the long term. For a long-term holding, IAU’s lower fee compared to GLD could accumulate to a tangible difference. For example, the next chart shows the returns from GLD and IAU in the past 10 years with an initial balance of $10,000. As seen, in this example, IAU would end with a balance of $30,386, outperforming GLD's $29,940 by $446. For investors holding larger positions, the difference would scale proportionally.

Other risks and final thoughts

Investing in GLD and IAU entails a common set of risks such as counterparty risk, tracking errors, etc. Here, I will focus less on these generic risks and risks that are more pertinent to the analysis presented in this article.

I argued why the U.S. credit rating downgrade has made the return/risk ratio more attractive for GLD and IAU. However, investors need to bear in mind that the downgrade could have also made the return/risk ratio of other asset classes more attractive too. As a notable example, the downgrade has caused fluctuations of treasury rates too, as you can see from the following figure. The 10-Year Treasury Rate (I:10YTR) rose past 4.5% afterward the downgrade was announced and currently sits at 4.58%. Treasury rates with longer maturity rose even more with 30-year treasury rates now hovering above 5% as of this writing.

I also want to get back to the issue of fees. IAU's fee of 0.25% is lower relative to GLD’s 0.40%. However, it's still quite high relative to large equity and/or bonds funds (which are as low as 0.03% these days). Even among gold ETFS, more recent additions such GLDM and IAU now offer significantly reduced fees in the range of ~0.10%. We do have sizable positions in IAU for both our growth and withdrawal accounts, as you can see from the following updates we recently provided in our Investing Group (Envision Early Retirement). These are our legacy IAU shares that we bought years back. We're still holding on to them as the switch costs (mostly taxes) far outweigh the fee savings. As communicate with our members, for new additions, we will go with GLDM.

To close, recent changes in our macroeconomic conditions have promoted me to reexamine IAU and GLD with a long-term orientation. I believe the recent U.S. credit downgrade will have long-lasting impacts on the dollar index and subsequently gold pricing. As such, I feel bullish on both IAU and GLD, and specially suggest investors with a long-term timeframe to consider IAU (or other gold funds with lower fees if you don’t trade often).

data.title: Palantir: Expect Major Sentiment Upside From The Golden Dome
data.description:
Since my last analysis of Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR), the stock is up by 4% in price. In my portfolio, my Palantir position is up by 53.1% at the time of this writing. I am not adding to my position at this time due to short-term overextended sentiment.

Palantir is positioned to change the modern defense landscape as part of the team behind the ambitious U.S. Golden Dome missile defense program—a visionary plan to intercept advanced missile threats using artificial intelligence and space technology. In collaboration with elite partners SpaceX (SPACE) and Anduril, Palantir will utilize its elite platforms to aggregate and analyze important satellite data, enabling timely and accurate responses to emerging threats. This represents a significant moment where Silicon Valley is boldly challenging established defense contractors. At this juncture, investors are presented with a unique opportunity to participate early on in Palantir's ascension as the integral AI component of intelligent-tech global security.

Golden Dome for a Golden Age

Palantir will be the intelligence backbone for the U.S. government’s proposed Golden Dome missile defense system—a Trump-supported program designed to intercept missiles from hypersonics to ICBMs using artificial intelligence and space technology. Key partners of Palantir include SpaceX, which has been assigned the task of deploying a constellation of 400–1,000+ tracking satellites, and Anduril Industries, which will provide autonomous drones and next-generation sensor payloads. Palantir’s platforms will collect satellite sensor data, use artificial intelligence to identify threats, and direct interceptor systems. The defense network would be delivered by way of subscription instead of through procurement.

Palantir's association with the Golden Dome is evidence of the evolution of the current defense landscape—it represents the emergence of Silicon Valley challenging established defense contractors in critical national security projects. Such a significant contract awarded to the SpaceX–Palantir–Anduril trio would be a substantial victory for technology innovators, while signifying the importance of rapid-scaling AI systems to provide modern and competitive solutions. Palantir will be part of a new wave of AI-first defense succeeding legacy contractors and keeping the West firmly on the offensive in terms of technological superiority over adversaries.

Financials & Return Potential

The Golden Dome will provide Palantir with substantial financial growth potential. The project, potentially involving a contract of around $175 billion over the medium to long term, begins with a $25 billion contract currently being processed by Congress. Palantir is the prime software provider for the SpaceX-led bid, which could see massive amounts of revenue growth through its data integration, AI, and command and control solutions. If Palantir is awarded even a very low single digit percentage of the project (either by contract or annual subscription), that would be in excess of several billions of dollars of revenue over time. Therefore, the expected ~$3.9B revenue base in FY 2025 will see a significant boost.

The subscription-based model suggested in the Golden Dome partnership has the potential to shift Palantir's revenues from standard government contracts to subscription-based, high-margin, recurring-revenue deals. The Pentagon will rationally have concerns about enduring ongoing costs, but a subscription model provides higher service quality in the long run and ongoing development. Ultimately, a thriving U.S. macroeconomy (which is founded on the sustainable growth of the country’s own corporate leaders, like Palantir) is just as important to national security as defense budgets are.

Large government contracts, like the Golden Dome, can truly boost Palantir’s profitability. The company is now GAAP profitable with a 20% operating margin. These long-term, high-margin contracts will demand extensive upfront software development, and once the software is in production, the subscription revenue has minimal incremental cost, which increases margins over time. Also, Palantir has already shown success in the market handling large contracts such as its $1.3B U.S. Army Maven AI system, and management is clearly masterful at scaling revenue efficiently. The key point is that as software reuse becomes more prominent in the Golden Dome subscription model, Palantir's margin on the project can increase further, vastly offsetting their initial investments in R&D.

In all cases in the above table, Palantir's status as an integrator in long-term defense programs like the Golden Dome will add revenue stability that routinely would not be found in high-growth tech companies. Even in a bear-case scenario, having steady government contracts and renewals would provide added stability around innovation and investment in R&D. The bull case speaks to a more powerful upside potential, should Palantir achieve entrenched status as the AI-defense infrastructure for Western nations and its broader allies (India, Asia-Pacific allies—speculative at this stage); this is plausible given the international U.S.-led agenda of global interdependence the current administration is operating. However, it should still be considered that Palantir's current valuation (GAAP TTM P/E >500) has already built in a great deal of optimism, so the company will need to execute flawlessly to justify and sustain market sentiment.

Sentiment Volatility Drivers

Congressional approval on defense funding will be a significant driver of sentiment for Palantir's prospects. If Congress acts quickly to approve the proposed funding for the Golden Dome missile defense contract (the currently proposed "big beautiful bill" is $25B), then investor sentiment will likely improve. If, however, the budget is delayed, or cut back, it will increase concern on revenue growth not being as high as anticipated. As Palantir has recently outlined its FY2025 revenue guidance (+36%, largely due to strong demand for AI), investors will be looking for information in upcoming guidance related to any shifts in government spending.

Palantir's current valuation relies heavily on its ability to deliver on large projects. Achieving milestones in a timely manner would only enhance investor optimism for the stock. This includes prototyping a satellite-based "custody layer" (a dedicated network of satellites that maintain continuous tracking) of missile threats from launch to interception by 2026, in conjunction with Anduril and SpaceX. However, delays, overruns, technical issues, or missile-tracking reliability issues could quickly sour investor interest and rapidly compress Palantir’s valuation. Given the currently elevated state of the company's stock, any negative news related to something like a lost contract or feasibility concerns could have an outsized impact on the stock in the short term.

While Palantir is a defense contractor, investors are likely to associate it more broadly with the AI industry. Therefore, its stock price may increase or decrease based on investor sentiment regarding tech and AI. For example, while sustained investor enthusiasm for AI will support valuations for Palantir, any downturn (like the bursting of an "AI bubble" related to Big Tech funding cuts or slower AI-related capex scaling), could have a negative impact on the market's preferences for high multiple stocks, including Palantir. A high valuation places Palantir more at risk in these sentiment-moving events. However, you should seriously consider that greater volatility is the price of long-term excellence in portfolio returns. Dips in stable companies' valuations that have strong management and incredible technology moats is a reason to add to positions, not sell. Always invest with the macro thesis, in which Palantir is front-and-center of long-term global security amid international democratic alliance consolidation.

Long-Term Outlook

By 2030, should Palantir execute effectively, it could become as critical to the defense of the West as GPS or the internet (crucial, yet in Palantir's case, almost imperceptible). Military leaders may rely on Palantir dashboards to make real-time strategic decisions, as they currently depend on their own radar or satellite image analysis for such instances. Palantir software would coordinate multi-domain operations seamlessly that encompass not only cyber defense, but also satellite targeting and conventional military responses, in order to provide one unified operational plan that leverages bleeding-edge AI. In this role, Palantir would be irreplaceable, effectively establishing itself as a critical defense utility. Eventually, it will likely become a dividend powerhouse.

Economically, I’d liken Palantir's evolution to Salesforce's (CRM) evolution into enterprise Customer Relationship Management—I could imagine Palantir evolving into the "central command platform" for governments. Except, I think Palantir's management will make the company's moat and market position much longer-lasting than Salesforce's, not least because of the high-barrier defense relationships and highly sensitive commercial-sector network effects. By 2030, I estimate Palantir's only real competitors would come from a disruptive change in intelligent technology itself (another fundamental technological revolution—unlikely in our lifetimes, though with AI nothing is off the table).

Palantir's long-term focus, supported by ultra-aware founders Alex Karp and Peter Thiel, supports the buy-and-hold bull case.

Portfolio Positioning

Seeking Alpha investors with a horizon of more than five years should consider Palantir as a core holding in their "Next Generation Defense and AI" investment strategy. Palantir is uniquely well-positioned, using its first-mover advantage in expanding markets. The company is generating momentum from its recent major wins (Golden Dome, NATO, and major commercial contracts). Palantir is the only company that I am aware of that combines a specialized approach to defense, heavy investment in bleeding-edge AI, and is of a larger scale in this cross-market above cloud providers, traditional defense primes, or smaller AI businesses. This suggests that Palantir is well-situated for sustained long-term growth.

Moreover, I think investors should be aggressively optimistic in weighting Palantir in portfolios, while sizing positions carefully due to the fact Palantir is more volatile than a larger stable mega-cap position (perhaps about half, or even ⅔, of capital compared to a mega-cap position is wise).

Palantir shares may remain volatile for a while as the valuation is still up for debate. However, more sophisticated investors should put greater emphasis on core metrics related to Palantir's government deal pipeline, commercial customer additions, net dollar retention, and marginal improvements. If you see a growing deal backlog, growing customer base (with the Fortune 500 companies like we see reported recently), and improving operating margins, then you should be supportive of Palantir's strategic narrative and associated valuation in the interim. However, if government revenue begins to slow down, I will be looking for signs that traction is slowing more generally (i.e., by tracking international expansion in customer base, growth in U.S. government contracts, etc.). Currently, I do think there are favorable trends that indicate Palantir's growth narrative remains fully intact for the indefinite future. I find the downside risks in terms of pure operational traction to be negligible.

Conclusion: Buy

Palantir’s engagement with the Golden Dome project showcases the company as a once-in-a-generation defense contractor, in an era where software will be as critical as hardware. The combination of the company’s extensive ties to national security with the advent of a scalable subscription-based model is distinct. Given Palantir stock’s current price, there is little room for error in execution, but investment gains could be substantial if management keeps the organization on a hyper-successful track through 2030. For patient investors willing to accept both volatility and political sensitivity, Palantir is an attractive, asymmetric bet with the potential for outsized returns as AI becomes ever more pivotal in a world where intelligence-driven, software-defined advantages will shape global dynamics.
